item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I'm surprised at the number of commentors who seem to have missed something really important here
- The son isn't the reality bender, and this is one of the sadder skips I've ever read.

Put together the breadcrumbs and you get strong hints that Dave and Sarah separated and his son went to live with her- decades ago.

Whether he's got dementia from years of drinking, broken by rejected attempts at reconnecting with his estranged family, suffering from the early stages of Alzheimer's, or some combination thereof, this is all a misguided effort to be like the guy his kid idolized back when he knew him.

Whether it's reality bending or memory alteration, he's trying so hard to rewrite the past that it's working.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: This image is non-compliant because it is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 2.0, which is not compliant with CC-BY-SA 3.0. As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly? I don't see anything in it that makes me believe it's reality altering. It seems like it could just as easily be causing the viewers to have incorrect memories rather than creating new realities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed it, especially when I got very angry that crocs use to be tiny and cute and not death machines.

One note, it seems like his kid would be SCP-3413-B right? Like, perhaps it's him making all this shit real? Or at least, a comment about him be proposed to be -B and him being a priority to contain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed the article. +1

There is a little inconsistency here in the pronouns used for SCP-3413-A:

He [SCP-3413-A] appears in clothes typical of an Amazonian safari, and speaks with an exaggerated Australian accent. As the host of SCP-3413, it [SCP-3413-A] provides information regarding the featured animal- including habitat, diet, and methods of survival within their own ecosystem.

I kind of think this should be a Keter, since the Foundation has not discovered a way to prevent it from altering reality, and the containment procedures merely cover up what it has done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It might be because the last interview just explains what we already know in a different way, never adding anything. Good scp apart from this detail

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so where's the youtube channel then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the crew (sans Lester) have some sort of reality-altering abilities, and are taking pity on faux-Irwin by making his son's fantasies come to life? That's what the interview log seemed to imply

Anyway god damn I liked this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who else tried to find the video on youtube?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this. Felt like the "livestream" bit was too long and got kinda boring. I think the prior dialogue with the cameraman already executed the exposition part perfectly. No need to extend it.

With that said, I still love the article. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great and I like it a lot. Minor biologist gripe - Cheetahs are only one species, there aren't other kinds.

Unless Safari Dave invented new ones, of course.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The core concept is cute: the son is a reality bender who causes reality to be changed to fit the descriptions in the show.

But this article is about three or four times longer than it needs to be. I would delete most of the dialog bits and let the core of the article speak for itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sign me up, I'm gonna go work with Safari Dave :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: coulda been scavengers before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given what we know about the anomaly, I don't really understand why the documentation includes the claim that Dave's documentaries are altering reality. The article strongly implies that Dave is just muddling listeners' memories, and given how incredibly lax the containment procedures are the Foundation presumably believes this on some level. If the description as written was true, Dave could cause nigh-apocalyptic scenarios with an offhanded comment about housecats or mosquitos, which would be far too risky to leave unaddressed. The article only makes sense as written if the Foundation is either dangerously negligent or making a transparent attempt to obfuscate the truth for no obvious benefit. I can't get behind either interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm tired, I liked it, I think the kid might have a connection with 239, I think I'm using the letter "I" to much, I'm done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And don't forget that they are twenty meters long, with steel proboscises!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only gripe I had about this is that it seems like the videos altering reality seems to be a pretty big conclusion to jump to given that their only clue is that people who see the videos remember things differently. +1 otherwise

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this as a draft, and I love it now. It's a great first contribution, and I'm both glad to see it succeeding and excited to see anything else you come up with. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a wonderful first entry. I particularly like the use of slightly more obscure animals so as to force ignoramuses like me to Google to make sure you didn't make them up, just like Dave's son. +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Here we see a honey bear not engaged in a life or death struggle about anything. These honey bears are placid and peaceful creatures and consequently bad television."

(Monty Python, "Life and Death Struggles".)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it must be that the prey-animals also can only run about as fast as humans. Maybe we never saw that episode though ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the article, but one thing screwed with my suspension of disbelief. If cheetahs only ran as fast as humans they'd all starve to death. They couldn't catch any prey at all. Other big cats lean towards 50 mph during a sprint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm undecided. It's competently written but I wasn't too impressed with some of the awkward phrasing. Particular areas didn't feel clinical enough.

As SCP-3413's host, he provides information about the featured animal — including their habitats, eating habits, and how they survive in their ecosystem. Although most of the information is accurate, some episodes include an incorrect statement regarding the subject.

This is nitpicky, but the repetition of 'habit' doesn't read well, and I'm slightly baffled as to why you chose the term 'eating habits' over 'diet'? You're also using he/him pronouns for what should likely be classified as an it. Here's how I'd personally rewrite this for a colder feel.

As the host of SCP-3413, it provides information regarding the featured animal- including habitat, diet, and methods of survival within their own ecosystem. The majority of this information is accurate, however, occasional episodes include falsified or otherwise incorrect statements.

+ more stuff
- close

Correct me if I'm wrong, but there has to be an account uploading these videos, yes? I find it odd that the Foundation wouldn't make any mention of this account, attempt to track its IP, etc- perhaps it was an oversight on your part or perhaps you just didn't think it mattered, but I was surprised and a little aggravated that there wasn't even a hand-wavey explanation such as 'the IP didn't exist.'

Let's talk concept. From what I can gather, Safari Dave is a broken man who wishes to impress/gain the affection of his now presumably adult son. He attempts this via creating a bunch of videos parodying his kid's favourite TV show. But- then, for me at least, it got kind of muddied. I can't tell whether the intention is that it's ambiguous or whether there is a definitive answer, but I had two questions in mind immediately after the read.

1. Is this a universe in which these animals are actually these falsified versions to begin with and are altered to be as we currently understand them by the content of the video (as the interview with the unknown party would suggest)?
or 2. People just perceive Dave's information as incorrect and shit all over him regardless (as the interview with Lester would suggest)?

I feel like an explanation is warranted. Are the Foundation being punked by this anomaly? That's the feel I'm getting, but it doesn't make any sense, because they would then be contradicting themselves within the article. Perhaps that's the point. Perhaps I don't get it. Let's move on.

I have a few more miscellaneous irks and issues that I'll quickfire off.

  • Why do the Foundation go into such detail describing sun bears within the Video Logs section?
  • Why would a Researcher be sent to psychological counselling and interrogation for misspelling a word? I get that you try to excuse it by making them 'one specializing in ungulates', but that comes across as quite hacky and doesn't really neutralize the absurdity of the situation. They can still make mistakes. They're not indestructible knowledge machines. It's silly.
  • Why would the Foundation not press for further information when confronting Lester? They couldn't get bank statements from the payment he received and track the sender? They couldn't ask for e-mails from his employer or business cards or even the slightest piece of evidence that could assist them in finding Dave? That's sloppy, and too crude a procedure for my tastes.

I would have really liked an interview from the son. He would be near impossible to find, but I think it would inject some much-needed clarity into the situation. For now, whilst the individual errors aren't terrible, they so collectively form a pretty choppy and confusing piece of work. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's bad enough that your blood boils when they bite you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems really familiar. I think I have read an SCP about videos that change reality before but I don't remember it's number or much else about it. Does anybody remember the SCP I am talking about?

Edit: Maybe it was deleted. I searched though the SCPs tagged "online" and didn't find it. I might search through a few other tags later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Imagine if he started making stuff up about something like a mosquito…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The title alone is upvote worthy, the actual article was just a bonus.

In all seriousness though, that was really enjoyable. Perfect balance between giving clues to what is going on without spelling it out unnecessarily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those heavily concerning SCPs, what if the kid gets too imaginative and creates something really really strange?

This is really fucking good and my most favorite part was the TROBLE video.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems as though this guy seems to be causing a Mandala effect with these videos. Pretty okay, so I'll give it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this as a draft. +1 easy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. It has a Series 2 vibe since it's not something complicated buried under lots of logs and high stakes. Simple but leaves a great impact on the reader.

I wonder if he was always like this or because of age his mind deteriorated. Very sad and here's my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could probably do without that last interview entirely to be honest, but this was good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: kisses

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you're welcome for the cheetah joke you maniac

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The video title refers to animals in the genus Giraffa as "giraffes". According to affected persons, the correct spelling is "girraffes"

I upvoted at this point. Nothing made me change my mind afterwards. Great stuff.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3413, "Irwin Shrugged".

I promise I'll find those sun bears for you, kid.

Special Thanks: fieldstone, Soulless, hippo, taylor_itkin, Mendellsohn, MaliceAF, Superblobby, and everyone in the Character Scramble discord for critiquing my story and making the original (bad) draft into something I can be proud of. If I've forgotten anybody, let me know. It took a lot of work and a lot of group effort, but I'm happy that I've finally gotten my first SCP mainlisted.

Image Credits: "Tongue Twister" by Valerie, licensed under CC by 2.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, and i want to upvote it. My major gripe is that it ends rather flat. Leaving the reader wanting more is not a bad thing, but i can't help but feel this ends without any sort of warning or payoff.

I'm going to novote tentative upvote for now, but I'll be checking back

Edit: this could also just be me
Edit: apparently it is
